meta.name: City of Portland
meta.description: Largest city in Oregon. Known for sustainability leadership, urban growth boundary, and progressive climate policy. Home to a major tech sector and significant houselessness crisis.

social.name: food
social.value: 1 in 4 Oregonians food insecure; Multnomah County ~14%
social.context: Oregon Food Bank distributed 50M+ meals in 2023. Multnomah County food insecurity ~14%, driven by high cost of living. Portland has strong community garden and farm-to-table culture but significant disparities in east Portland.
actions: Shop at Portland Farmers Markets to support local agriculture

social.name: health
social.context: Oregon expanded Medicaid (Oregon Health Plan) covering ~1.4M residents statewide. Portland's uninsured rate of 5.9% is below the national average (8.0%). Mental health and substance abuse are significant challenges, especially among houseless population.
social.source: U.S. Census Bureau ACS via Data USA
actions: Volunteer with Outside In or Central City Concern

social.name: education
social.context: Portland Public Schools graduation rate of 83.6% is near the state average (82.5%). Significant achievement gaps by race: White 88%, Black 72%, Hispanic 74%, Native 63%. Strong higher education access with PSU, Reed, Lewis & Clark.
actions: Volunteer as a tutor with SMART Reading
Mentor students through Big Brothers Big Sisters

social.name: income & work
social.context: Median household income $88,792 but 14% in poverty. Cost of living index 130 (30% above national average). Tech sector creates high-wage jobs but service sector wages lag. Gig economy and housing costs strain working families.
social.source: U.S. Census Bureau ACS via Data USA

social.name: housing
social.context: Portland-metro 2024 PIT count: ~6,300 unhoused, one of highest per capita on West Coast. Median property value $557,600. Homeownership 52.8%. Voters approved $652M housing bond. Rent stabilization (10%+CPI cap) since 2019. 47% of renters cost-burdened.
social.source: Multnomah County Joint Office of Homeless Services; Census ACS
actions: Volunteer at shelters (Blanchet House, Union Gospel Mission)

social.name: water & sanitation
social.value: Meets all EPA standards; Bull Run watershed protected
social.context: Portland's water comes from the Bull Run watershed (unfiltered surface water) and Columbia South Shore wellfield. Consistently meets all federal standards. $500M filtration plant under construction (required by EPA LT2 rule). Lead service line replacement program underway.
actions: Check your home for lead pipes — free testing available

social.name: energy
social.context: Oregon's grid is ~40% renewable, dominated by hydropower. Portland General Electric committed to 100% clean by 2040. Average residential rate ~12¢/kWh (below national avg). Portland Clean Energy Fund (PCEF) — voter-approved 1% surcharge on large retailers — funds clean energy projects in frontline communities. $116M+ distributed.
social.source: Portland General Electric; Portland Clean Energy Fund
actions: Switch to PGE Green Future renewable option
Install solar — Oregon has 25% state tax credit

social.name: social equity
social.context: Gini of 0.48 indicates significant inequality. Portland has a history of exclusionary zoning and redlining (Albina neighborhood). East Portland has lower incomes, fewer services, more renters. Racial wealth gap is stark: median white household wealth ~10x Black household wealth.
actions: Advocate for equitable development in east Portland
Engage with the Portland Clean Energy Fund equity work

social.name: peace & justice
social.context: Portland saw a spike in violent crime 2020-2022, peaking at 101 homicides in 2022. Down to 68 in 2023. Property crime remains high. Gun violence disproportionately affects communities of color. Police staffing crisis (lowest per capita of any major US city).
actions: Engage with Portland Street Response (alternative to police for crisis)

social.name: political voice
social.context: Oregon pioneered vote-by-mail (since 2000) and automatic voter registration (2016). Portland turnout consistently above state average. 2023: Portland adopted ranked-choice voting and new 4-district council system (first election Nov 2024).
social.source: Multnomah County Elections; Oregon Secretary of State
actions: Vote in every election — ballots come by mail
Attend City Council meetings (new district-based system)
Support voter engagement in underrepresented communities

social.name: gender equality
social.value: Women earn 88¢ per $1 earned by men
social.context: Oregon's Equal Pay Act (2017) is among the strongest in the US, prohibiting salary history inquiries. Portland metro gender pay ratio ~0.88. Gap wider for women of color (Latina women: 0.54, Black women: 0.64). Oregon was first state to pass equal pay legislation (1955).
social.source: Bureau of Labor Statistics; Oregon Bureau of Labor and Industries
actions: Support pay transparency in your workplace
Advocate for enforcement of Oregon Equal Pay Act
Mentor women and non-binary individuals in your field

social.name: networks
social.context: Portland has strong broadband infrastructure with multiple ISPs. City-owned fiber backbone (IRNE) serves institutional users. Digital equity gap persists in east Portland and among low-income households. City Digital Equity Action Plan adopted 2023.
social.source: City of Portland Office for Community Technology
actions: Donate devices to Free Geek Portland

ecological.name: climate change
ecological.target: Net zero by 2050; 50% reduction from 1990 by 2030
ecological.context: Portland+Multnomah County emissions peaked in 2000, now 38% below 1990 levels (as of 2022). Transportation ~40%, buildings ~35%, industrial ~15%. Oregon's Climate Protection Program (CPP) caps fossil fuel emissions. Portland declared climate emergency 2020.
ecological.source: City of Portland Bureau of Planning & Sustainability
actions: Ride TriMet, bike, or walk — transportation is 40% of emissions
Switch to an EV — Oregon offers $2,500 rebate + federal credit

ecological.name: ocean acidification
ecological.context: Oregon coast experiences severe seasonal upwelling that brings naturally acidic deep water to the surface. Combined with CO2 absorption, pH drops threaten Dungeness crab and oyster industries. Oregon Ocean Acidification Task Force formed 2019.
ecological.source: Oregon State University / NOAA Pacific Marine Environmental Lab
actions: Reduce carbon footprint — CO2 drives acidification
Support Oregon ocean acidification research funding

ecological.name: chemical pollution
ecological.value: Portland Harbor Superfund (10-mile stretch of Willamette)
ecological.target: Complete Superfund cleanup; reduce TRI releases
ecological.context: Portland Harbor is one of the largest Superfund sites in the US — 10 miles of the Willamette River contaminated by decades of industrial pollution (PCBs, heavy metals, pesticides). EPA cleanup plan approved 2017, estimated $1B+ over 13 years. Industrial TRI releases declining.
actions: Follow fish consumption advisories for the Willamette River
Support Portland Harbor cleanup advocacy (Willamette Riverkeeper)
Support Right to Know initiatives for toxic releases

ecological.name: nitrogen & phosphorus loading
ecological.value: Moderate — agricultural runoff in Willamette Basin
ecological.context: Willamette Basin has nutrient pollution from agricultural runoff (primarily upstream of Portland). Portland's stormwater management (Green Streets, bioswales) has reduced urban nutrient runoff significantly. Combined sewer overflow (CSO) largely resolved by $1.4B Big Pipe project (2011).
ecological.source: Oregon DEQ / City of Portland Bureau of Environmental Services
actions: Support Friends of Trees and watershed council work
Properly maintain septic systems if applicable

ecological.name: freshwater withdrawals
ecological.value: Bull Run watershed: ample supply with climate risks
ecological.target: Sustainable water supply under climate scenarios
ecological.context: Portland's Bull Run watershed provides high-quality unfiltered water from 102 sq mi of protected forest. Annual rainfall ~135 inches. Climate models project summer flows declining 10-30% by 2050. Columbia South Shore wellfield provides backup. $500M filtration plant under construction.
actions: Conserve water during summer dry season

ecological.name: land conversion
ecological.value: UGB limits sprawl; 16,000+ acres of parks/natural areas
ecological.target: Maintain UGB; increase urban canopy to 33%
ecological.context: Oregon's Urban Growth Boundary (UGB, since 1973) is nationally unique — limits development sprawl. Metro manages the Portland-area UGB. Forest Park (5,200 acres) is one of largest urban forests in US. Urban tree canopy ~31%. Metro bond measures protect natural areas.
ecological.source: Metro Regional Government; Portland Parks & Recreation
actions: Support Friends of Trees planting events
Volunteer in Forest Park with Forest Park Conservancy
Support Metro natural areas bond measures
Advocate for infill development over UGB expansion

ecological.name: biodiversity loss
ecological.value: Salmon runs declining; urban habitat fragmented
ecological.target: Recover ESA-listed salmon; protect native habitats
ecological.context: Multiple salmon and steelhead runs in the Willamette/Columbia are ESA-listed. Portland has restored significant urban habitat (Crystal Springs, Oaks Bottom, Johnson Creek). Climate change and urban development pressure native species. 200+ bird species recorded in Portland.
ecological.source: Oregon Department of Fish & Wildlife; Portland Audubon
actions: Plant native species in your yard

ecological.name: air pollution
ecological.context: Meets EPA standard (12 µg/m³) but exceeds revised WHO guideline (5). Wildfire smoke is the dominant concern — Sept 2020 smoke event was worst air quality in the world (AQI 500+). 2023 Canadian wildfire smoke also impacted Portland. Industrial emissions from NW Portland (Esco foundry) are a local environmental justice concern.
actions: Check AQI daily during fire season (airnow.gov)
Use HEPA filters during smoke events
Advocate for industrial emission reductions in NW Portland

ecological.name: ozone layer depletion
ecological.context: Montreal Protocol success story. Ozone layer recovering globally. Local contribution minimal.
actions: Properly dispose of old refrigerants and AC units
